sections.title: Purpose & Scope
sections.content:
This procedure describes the decontamination process for the ambulance or response vehicle following transport of or attendance to a patient with a known or suspected infectious condition involving blood, body fluid exposure, or a notifiable infectious disease.

Decontamination must be completed before the vehicle is returned to service. An inadequately decontaminated vehicle presents an infection risk to subsequent patients, to the clinician, and to others entering the vehicle. In a remote FIFO setting where environmental services may not be available, the attending clinician is responsible for completing decontamination to the required standard.

sections.title: When Decontamination Is Required
sections.content:
Decontamination using this enhanced protocol is required following:

- Any patient with a known or suspected notifiable infectious disease (e.g., active tuberculosis, viral haemorrhagic fever, meningococcal disease, COVID-19 or equivalent respiratory pathogen with aerosol-generating procedures performed)
- Any incident involving significant blood or body fluid contamination of vehicle surfaces, equipment, or stretcher
- Any patient with known or suspected multi-drug resistant organism (MRDO) colonisation or infection
- Any patient with active diarrhoea or vomiting where surfaces were contaminated
- Any situation where the attending clinician has a reasonable concern about infectious risk

Routine patient transport without the above features requires standard cleaning only; not this enhanced protocol.

sections.title: PPE Required
sections.content:
Full PPE must be worn before entering the vehicle for decontamination. Do not enter the vehicle without PPE in place:

- Gloves: heavy-duty or doubled nitrile gloves
- Gown: fluid-resistant disposable gown
- Mask: P2/N95 respirator for patients with suspected airborne transmission; surgical mask at minimum for all other infectious decontaminations
- Eye protection: goggles or face shield

PPE must be applied in the correct donning sequence per the PPE procedure before entering the vehicle. PPE must not be removed until you have exited the vehicle and completed the doffing sequence.

sections.title: Decontamination Process
sections.content:
Complete decontamination in the following sequence:

1. Remove all removable soft furnishings; blankets, pillow cases, mattress cover, and any cloth items that were in the patient compartment. Place in a sealed, labelled clinical laundry bag for laundering at appropriate temperature (minimum 60°C for 10 minutes).
2. Remove all disposable waste; bag and seal all single-use items, packaging, and clinical waste in a clinical waste bag.
3. Apply TGA-approved disinfectant; use a product with TGA registration as a hospital-grade disinfectant or an Organic Chlorine-based product (e.g., 1,000 ppm available chlorine for routine, 10,000 ppm for blood spills). Refer to the product label for the correct concentration and preparation.
4. Clean in a top-to-bottom, back-to-front sequence. Begin with the ceiling and light fittings, then work down. This prevents re-contamination of already cleaned surfaces.
5. Priority surfaces; stretcher (all surfaces, undercarriage, and wheels), side rails, stretcher locking mechanism, oxygen outlet and tubing, suction device and tubing, door handles (interior and exterior), grab rails, floor, and any surfaces the patient contacted.
6. Allow contact time; product must remain wet on the surface for the full contact time specified on the label (typically 5–10 minutes). Do not wipe off before contact time is complete.
7. Wipe surfaces clean after contact time with a fresh cloth or wipe.
8. Equipment decontamination; any equipment used during the call must be decontaminated per the manufacturer's instructions. Equipment that cannot be decontaminated to manufacturer standards must be treated as single-use and disposed of.
9. Ventilate the vehicle; leave doors open for at least 30 minutes after decontamination, particularly following patients with suspected airborne transmission.
10. Doff PPE outside the vehicle and perform thorough hand hygiene.

sections.title: Vehicle Out of Service
sections.content:
- The vehicle is out of service from the time decontamination begins until it is certified complete
- The vehicle must not be used for any patient transport during decontamination
- If the site has a second response vehicle, activate it immediately when the primary vehicle is placed out of service for decontamination
- If no backup vehicle is available, notify the site HSE manager and medical director immediately so alternative emergency response arrangements can be activated
- Do not rush decontamination to return the vehicle to service; an inadequate decontamination is worse than a delayed return to service
- The clinician completing decontamination must certify it as complete before the vehicle is returned to service

sections.title: Documentation
sections.content:
A decontamination record must be completed immediately after enhanced decontamination. The record must include:

- Date and time decontamination commenced and completed
- Reason for enhanced decontamination (nature of infectious risk)
- Surfaces and equipment cleaned
- Disinfectant product name, concentration used, and batch number
- Contact time achieved
- Any equipment disposed of as single-use
- Clinician name, signature, and AHPRA registration number
- Time vehicle returned to service

This record is retained in the vehicle logbook and in the patient's clinical record where relevant. For notifiable disease cases, the site infection control lead and medical director must also be notified.

sections.title: Regulatory Basis
sections.content:
- Therapeutic Goods Act 1989 (Cth): TGA disinfectant registration requirements
- Public Health Act 2016 (WA): notifiable disease requirements
- Australian Commission on Safety and Quality in Health Care: Preventing and Controlling Infections Standard
- AS/NZS 4187:2014: Reprocessing of reusable medical devices in health service organisations
- Ambulance Services Act 1982 (WA): vehicle standards for patient transport
